name: Bhai Gurmej Singh (Hazoori Raagi Amritsar)
body: Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji is one of the senior Raagis around today to have served in Sri Harmandir Sahib for decades. He is known for his sweet and mellow style of Kirtan that connects everyone to the Shabad. Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji was born on 10th September 1940 to Mata Raj Kaur and Sardar Parsa Singh in the village of Bajidpur, Jalandhar. His family was involved in farming and they led a moderate lifestyle. At the age of 8, he contracted ‘chicken pox’ and as a result of the illness, he lost his vision. Doctors were unable to treat him but these odds did not break the spirit of this young boy. Following the advice of Sant Baba Dal Singh Ji, he got enrolled in the Central Khalsa Orphanage, Amritsar in 1950 to learn Kirtan. Bhai Harnam Singh Jogi was teaching Kirtan at the orphanage during this period, so Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji learned the art of Gurmat Sangeet from him. At the same time, he also learned the Braille writing system from Giani Lal Singh Ji and he tried to memorize as much Gurbani as possible. He was also taught carpentry at the orphanage to add on to his vocational skills. Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji continued to learn Kirtan at the orphanage for 8 years and during this time he practiced rigorously to uphold the high standards of Kirtan being taught then.

He completed his Kirtan education in 1958 and his first Kirtan service was in Gurdwara Sahib Nanaksar Verka, Amritsar where he served for 2 years, between 1958 to 1960. Then, he went on to serve for 11 years in Gurdwara Sahib 7 Chander Road, Dehradun, from 1960 to 1971. In 1965, he got married to Bibi Balbir Kaur Ji and together, they are blessed with 2 sons and 2 daughters. In 1971, Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji left Dehradun and began his service in Sri Harmandir Sahib, Amritsar. He performed Kirtan in Darbar Sahib for 28 years until his retirement in 1998. During this time, the late Bhai Nirmal Singh Khalsa had the opportunity to accompany him on the side harmonium for a few years. In his book, ‘Gurmat Sangeet De Anmol Rattan’, Bhai Nirmal Singh Ji states that he learned a lot from Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji during their time together. He was taught to perform Kirtan with ‘Sehaj’ and to allow emphasis on the Shabad. One quality of Bhai Sahib Ji that he wrote about was his ability to memorize Gurbani. He would ask Bhai Nirmal Singh Ji to recite a Shabad, and after listening to the entire Shabad once or twice, it would be permanently rooted in his memory.

Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji has contributed a lot to the visually impaired members of the Sikh community. Due to the difficulties he faced in learning Gurbani, it was his wish that others would not face such challenges. So he took it upon himself to provide many resources in Braille. Some of his publications in Braille include Sukhmani Sahib in 1969, Bani of Guru Tegh Bahadur Sahib Ji in 1975, Nitnem in 1979, Asa Di Vaar with Shabads in 1981 and Bhagtan Di Bani in 1983. Subsequently, he undertook one of his biggest projects after retiring from his service at Sri Harmandir Sahib. In 2000, he began to transliterate the entire Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji into Braille and this project took him twelve years to complete. The first Braille copy was completed in 2012 and it has 2,153 pages spread across 18 volumes. This copy was presented to the visually impaired children at Cental Khalsa orphanage where he had spent his childhood. Hailing from a family with a background in farming, Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji has sowed a different kind of seed and the fruits of his labor will always be remembered.

Bhai Gurmej Singh has received numerous awards throughout his career including the Bhai Santa Singh Shiromani Raagi Award in 1988 and the Sant Baba Sucha Singh Ji award from Jawaddi Kalan in 1998. If anyone has more recordings of Bhai Gurmej Singh Ji to share, please contact us.
